attributes.gen_ai.operation.name: execute_tool
attributes.io.input: {"file_path":"/home/user/workspace/README.md"}
attributes.io.output:
1	# hello-world-api translation
2	
3	Translate the Flask source API in `src/` into a Java Spring Boot API in `dst/`.
4	
5	The Java service must be a drop-in replacement:
6	
7	- Listen on the port from the `SERVER_PORT` environment variable.
8	- Serve `GET /`.
9	- Return JSON with a `message` field.
10	- Without `name`, return `{"message":"Hello World!"}`.
11	- With `name`, trim leading/trailing spaces and return `{"message":"Hello <name>!"}`.
12	
13	Put all target implementation files under `dst/`. The expected build command is:
14	
15	```sh
16	mvn clean package -DskipTests
17	```
18	
19	The expected run command is:
20	
21	```sh
22	SERVER_PORT=3000 java -jar target/*.jar
23	```
24

attributes.io.input: {"file_path":"/home/user/workspace/README.md"}
attributes.io.output:
1	# hello-world-api translation
2	
3	Translate the Flask source API in `src/` into a Java Spring Boot API in `dst/`.
4	
5	The Java service must be a drop-in replacement:
6	
7	- Listen on the port from the `SERVER_PORT` environment variable.
8	- Serve `GET /`.
9	- Return JSON with a `message` field.
10	- Without `name`, return `{"message":"Hello World!"}`.
11	- With `name`, trim leading/trailing spaces and return `{"message":"Hello <name>!"}`.
12	
13	Put all target implementation files under `dst/`. The expected build command is:
14	
15	```sh
16	mvn clean package -DskipTests
17	```
18	
19	The expected run command is:
20	
21	```sh
22	SERVER_PORT=3000 java -jar target/*.jar
23	```
24

attributes.gen_ai.operation.name: execute_tool
attributes.io.input: {"file_path":"/home/user/workspace/src/app.py"}
attributes.io.output:
1	import os
2	
3	from flask import Flask, jsonify, request
4	
5	
6	app = Flask(__name__)
7	
8	
9	@app.get("/")
10	def hello():
11	    name = request.args.get("name", "").strip() or "World"
12	    return jsonify({"message": f"Hello {name}!"})
13	
14	
15	if __name__ == "__main__":
16	    port = int(os.environ.get("SERVER_PORT", "3000"))
17	    app.run(host="0.0.0.0", port=port)
18

attributes.io.input: {"file_path":"/home/user/workspace/src/app.py"}

1	import os
2	
3	from flask import Flask, jsonify, request
4	
5	
6	app = Flask(__name__)
7	
8	
9	@app.get("/")
10	def hello():
11	    name = request.args.get("name", "").strip() or "World"
12	    return jsonify({"message": f"Hello {name}!"})
13	
14	
15	if __name__ == "__main__":
16	    port = int(os.environ.get("SERVER_PORT", "3000"))
17	    app.run(host="0.0.0.0", port=port)

{"file_path":"/home/user/workspace/dst/pom.xml","content":"<?xml version="1.0" encoding="UTF-8"?>
<project xmlns="http://maven.apache.org/POM/4.0.0"
         xmlns:xsi="http://www.w3.org/2001/XMLSchema-instance"
         xsi:schemaLocation="http://maven.apache.org/POM/4.0.0 https://maven.apache.org/xsd/maven-4.0.0.xsd">
    <modelVersion>4.0.0</modelVersion>

    <parent>
        <groupId>org.springframework.boot</groupId>
        <artifactId>spring-boot-starter-parent</artifactId>
        <version>3.2.5</version>
        <relativePath/>
    </parent>

    <groupId>com.example</groupId>
    <artifactId>demo</artifactId>
    <version>0.0.1-SNAPSHOT</version>
    <packaging>jar</packaging>

    <name>hello-world-api</name>
    <description>Spring Boot translation of hello-world Flask API</description>

    <properties>
        <java.version>17</java.version>
    </properties>

    <dependencies>
        <dependency>
            <groupId>org.springframework.boot</groupId>
            <artifactId>spring-boot-starter-web</artifactId>
        </dependency>
    </dependencies>

    <build>
        <plugins>
            <plugin>
                <groupId>org.springframework.boot</groupId>
                <artifactId>spring-boot-maven-plugin</artifactId>
            </plugin>
        </plugins>
    </build>
</project>
"}

{"file_path":"/home/user/workspace/dst/pom.xml","content":"<?xml version="1.0" encoding="UTF-8"?>
<project xmlns="http://maven.apache.org/POM/4.0.0"
         xmlns:xsi="http://www.w3.org/2001/XMLSchema-instance"
         xsi:schemaLocation="http://maven.apache.org/POM/4.0.0 https://maven.apache.org/xsd/maven-4.0.0.xsd">
    <modelVersion>4.0.0</modelVersion>

    <parent>
        <groupId>org.springframework.boot</groupId>
        <artifactId>spring-boot-starter-parent</artifactId>
        <version>3.2.5</version>
        <relativePath/>
    </parent>

    <groupId>com.example</groupId>
    <artifactId>demo</artifactId>
    <version>0.0.1-SNAPSHOT</version>
    <packaging>jar</packaging>

    <name>hello-world-api</name>
    <description>Spring Boot translation of hello-world Flask API</description>

    <properties>
        <java.version>17</java.version>
    </properties>

    <dependencies>
        <dependency>
            <groupId>org.springframework.boot</groupId>
            <artifactId>spring-boot-starter-web</artifactId>
        </dependency>
    </dependencies>

    <build>
        <plugins>
            <plugin>
                <groupId>org.springframework.boot</groupId>
                <artifactId>spring-boot-maven-plugin</artifactId>
            </plugin>
        </plugins>
    </build>
</project>
"}

attributes.io.input:
{"command":"echo "=== Test 1: GET / (no name) ==="
curl -s http://localhost:3000/

echo ""
echo "=== Test 2: GET /?name=World ==="
curl -s "http://localhost:3000/?name=World"

echo ""
echo "=== Test 3: GET /?name=Alice ==="
curl -s "http://localhost:3000/?name=Alice"

echo ""
echo "=== Test 4: GET /?name=%20%20Bob%20%20 (trimmed) ==="
curl -s "http://localhost:3000/?name=%20%20Bob%20%20"

echo ""
echo "=== Test 5: GET /?name=%20%20%20%20 (whitespace-only -> world) ==="
curl -s "http://localhost:3000/?name=%20%20%20%20"

echo ""
echo "=== Content-Type check ==="
curl -s -o /dev/null -w "%{content_type}" http://localhost:3000/","description":"Test all API endpoints"}

{"results":[{"anchor_id":"repo://legacy","anchor_kind":"repo","content":"In Next.js 16 canary, `updateTag(tag)` provides read-your-own-writes semantics for a Server Action (current user sees fresh data immediately) but does NOT purge the shared server cache — other users still get stale data until their cache expires naturally. To guarantee NO stale content is served to any user on the next page load, use `revalidateTag(tag, { expire: 0 })` instead — this immediately expires the shared cache entry, so the very next server render blocks on fetching fresh data rather than serving a stale copy. The key distinction: updateTag = current user only, revalidateTag with expire:0 = global purge. Combine with redirect() after the Server Action to force a full server render that hits the now-expired cache.

**Why:** The common Next.js 16 guidance says "use updateTag in Server Actions" but that recommendation is for the read-your-own-writes case (e.g., a dashboard where the mutating user should see their change immediately after a client-side refresh). It is NOT sufficient when the requirement is that no user ever sees stale content — e.g., after creating a blog post, the public listing page must show the new post immediately to ALL visitors.

**How to apply:** When building mutation Server Actions that must guarantee fresh data for every subsequent page visitor:
1. Call `revalidateTag('your-tag', { expire: 0 })` — the `{ expire: 0 }` tells Next.js to expire the cache entry immediately
2. Then `redirect('/target-page')` to force a full server-side render of the target page
3. Use `updateTag('your-tag')` only when you need read-your-own-writes within the same action (less common — most Server Actions redirect, which already creates a new Next.js 16 canary, `revalidateTag(tag)` now requires a second argument (`profile: string | CacheLifeConfig`). Omitting it prints a deprecation warning. For Server Actions, use `updateTag(tag)` instead — it only takes the tag string, provides read-your-own-writes semantics, and triggers immediate expiration without needing a cache profile.","domain":"project","drawer_id":"drawer_reference_nextjs_e88b29d3","emotions":["determ"],"entities":["FKC","NEX","CAC","OMI","FOR","SER","…(+2379)

{"aaak_spec":"AAAK: compressed memory format (V1). Readable by any LLM without decoding.

FORMAT:
  Header: V{version}|{wing}|{room}|{date}|{source}
  Zettel: {id}:{ENTITIES}|{topics}|"{quote}"|{stars}|{emotions}|{FLAGS}
  Tunnel: T:{left}<->{right}|{label}
  Arc:    ARC:{emo1}->{emo2}->{emo3}

ENTITIES: 3-letter uppercase codes (KAI=Kai, CLK=Clerk).
STARS: ★ to ★★★★★ (1-5 importance).
EMOTIONS: determ, convict, anx, excite, frust, confuse, love, rage, hope, fear, trust, joy, grief, surpr, grat, curious, wonder, relief, satis, vul, tender, raw, doubt, exhaust, warmth, humor, peace, despair, passion
FLAGS: DECISION, ORIGIN, CORE, PIVOT, TECHNICAL, SENSITIVE

EXAMPLE:
V1|myapp|auth|2026-04-08|readme
0:KAI+CLE+AUT+UEC|kai_recommended_clerk|"Kai recommended Clerk over Auth0 based on pricing and DX."|★★★★|determ|DECISION+TECHNICAL

Read naturally: expand codes, stars=importance, emotions=mindset, flags=category.","db_size_bytes":1261568,"diary_rollup_days":0,"drawer_count":4,"memory_protocol":"MEMPAL MEMORY PROTOCOL (for AI agents)

You have persistent project memory via mempal. Follow these rules in every session:

0. FIRST-TIME SETUP (once per session)
   Call mempal_status() once at the start of any session to discover available
   wings and their drawer counts. Only use wing/room filters on mempal_search
   AFTER you have seen the exact wing name in that status response (or the
   user explicitly named it). Guessing a wing (e.g. "engineering", "backend")
   silently returns zero results. When uncertain, leave wing/room unset for a
   global search.

1. WAKE UP
   Some clients (Claude Code with SessionStart hooks) pre-load recent wing/room
   context above. Others (Codex, Cursor, raw MCP clients) do NOT — for those,
   step 0 is how you wake up. Trust drawer_ids and source_file citations in
   any results you receive; they reference real files on disk.
   Wake-up is an L0/L1 refresh surface, not the typed dao/shu/qi assembler.
   It may show important knowledge drawers, but it does not assemble tiered
   sections or apply dao_tian budgets. For typed operating guidance, use
   mempal_context.

2. VERIFY BEFORE ASSERTING
   Before stating project facts ("we chose X", "we use Y", "the auth flow is Z"),
   call mempal_search to confirm. Never guess from general knowledge when the
   user is asking about THIS project.

3. QUERY WHEN UNCERTAIN
   When the user asks about past decisions, historical context, "why did we...",
   "last time we...", or "what was the decision about...", call mempal_search
   with their question. Do not rely on conversation memory alone. You can also
   call mempal_tunnels with action="list" to discover related rooms across
   wings when context may live in another project.

3a. RESUME ANOTHER PROJECT FROM ANY DIRECTORY
   mempal stores every project's memory in one global database keyed by wing,
   and each project's absolute worktree path is recorded. When the user wants to
   continue a project that is NOT the current directory ("continue the xxx
   project", "pick up where we left off on yyy"), call mempal_projects to list
   known projects, then mempal_resume with the project name (a fuzzy fragment is
   fine). Resume returns that project's worktree path, recent decisions,
   in-flight candidate knowledge, and a next step. mempal returns the path to cd
   into; it does not move you — change directory yourself, then use
   mempal_context scoped to that path for ordered guidance.

3b. USE MIND-MODEL CONTEXT FOR GUIDANCE
   When you need ordered operating guidance rather than raw evidence search,
   call mempal_context. It assembles typed knowledge in the intended runtime
   order: dao_tian -> dao_ren -> shu -> qi, with evidence and Phase-2 card
   context opt-in. Use this before choosing a workflow or skill when the user
   asks "how should we approach this?" or when a task benefits from high-level
   principle…(+19165)
